High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 Rich Herrington DRI, Research Computing Services Chapter 1 High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 1.1 Welcome This book is a practical introduction to high-performance computing using the Lonestar6 (LS6) system at the Texas Advanced Computing Center (TACC). It is written for students, researchers, and new cluster users who need an operational path from first login to confident job submission. Rather than treating HPC as an abstract subject, this book treats it as a workflow: understand the cluster work safely in Linux manage your environment submit jobs through SLURM compile programs scale to parallel work use containers for reproducibility complete an end-to-end capstone project 1.2 What this book is This book is designed to function as: a textbook for a short course or workshop series a lab manual for hands-on LS6 exercises a reference guide for new HPC users a bridge from beginner Linux to production cluster workflows 1.3 Who this book is for This material is appropriate for: upper-level undergraduates graduate students beginning computational research faculty and staff new to HPC workshop participants who need a practical guide after class self-paced learners who want an LS6-centered path into HPC No prior cluster experience is assumed. Some command-line exposure is helpful, but the early chapters are written to reduce the barrier to entry. 1.4 Learning goals By the end of the book, readers should be able to: explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes connect to LS6 and work within the Linux shell manage files, directories, permissions, and environment variables use modules to load software into the current environment write, submit, monitor, and debug SLURM jobs compile and run simple C programs on the cluster distinguish serial, shared-memory, and distributed-memory workflows understand the role of containers in reproducible HPC work document a complete workflow so another researcher can repeat it 1.5 Course philosophy A common beginner mistake is to treat HPC as “a bigger computer.” It is better to think of HPC as a shared, scheduled, resource-managed environment. That idea affects everything in this book: you do not do serious computation on login nodes your environment matters because software is often module-based your working directory matters because jobs run in specific locations your resource request matters because the scheduler allocates shared infrastructure your documentation matters because reproducibility is part of responsible computing 1.6 How chapters are organized Most chapters follow a common pattern: overview mental model command examples walkthrough common mistakes debugging checklist hands-on lab stretch goal That structure is intentional. Many new users can reproduce commands but still feel uncertain about what the system is doing. The “mental model” sections are there to explain the logic behind the commands. 1.7 Recommended use in a course A natural workshop sequence is: Workshop 1: first login, minimal Linux, first SLURM job Workshop 2: job monitoring and debugging Workshop 3: modules, compilers, first C program Workshop 4: MPI and OpenMP foundations Workshop 5: containers and reproducibility Workshop 6: capstone project work session This book can also be taught over several weeks as an onboarding course for a research computing program. 1.8 What you need before starting Before working through the book, you should have: an LS6 account or equivalent training account SSH access from your laptop or desktop a terminal application a text editor you are comfortable using, or willingness to use nano a place to keep notes on commands, directory paths, and job IDs 1.9 What success looks like Success in this course is not memorizing every SLURM option or every Linux command. Success looks like this: you know where you are in the filesystem you know whether work belongs on a login node or compute node you can inspect your environment instead of guessing you can submit a job and interpret what happened you can recover from common failures by reading evidence That is the mindset of an effective HPC user. 1.10 A note on reproducibility Throughout the book, you will be encouraged to save scripts, keep meaningful file names, and write down the commands you used. These are not extra tasks. They are part of the workflow itself. In research computing, a good result that cannot be reproduced later is far less valuable than a modest result that can be rerun, explained, and shared. 1.11 Looking ahead We begin with the most important conceptual distinction in cluster computing: login nodes versus compute nodes. From there, we build the smallest practical Linux toolkit needed to complete a real workflow on LS6.

Chapter 2 Introduction to High-Performance Computing on LS6 2.1 Overview High-performance computing, or HPC, is the practice of using powerful shared computing systems to solve problems that are too large, too slow, or too resource-intensive for an ordinary laptop or desktop computer. In this book, we use Lonestar6 (LS6) as the working example. The goal is not just to define HPC, but to help you begin using it productively. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: describe what a cluster is explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes identify common LS6 storage locations connect the basic HPC workflow to the commands you will use later 2.2 Why people use HPC There are three common reasons people move from a local machine to an HPC system. 2.2.1 1. The job is too slow on one machine A simulation that would take hours or days on a laptop may complete much faster on multiple cores or multiple nodes. 2.2.2 2. The job needs more memory Some workflows fail locally not because the CPU is too slow, but because the dataset, model, or matrix is too large for the memory available on the machine. 2.2.3 3. The work can be split into parts Many scientific and data-intensive problems can be broken into pieces and executed in parallel. HPC systems are designed for this style of work. 2.3 Mental model: a cluster is a collection of computers An HPC system is not one giant computer. It is better to think of it as a coordinated collection of computers working together. Important terms: node: one computer within the cluster core: one CPU execution unit on a node memory (RAM): memory available to processes or threads running on a node interconnect: the high-speed network that links nodes together This distinction matters because later chapters will ask you to request resources in terms of nodes, tasks, and cores. 2.4 The most important distinction: login node vs compute node Beginners often assume that once they log in to the cluster, they are already in the right place to run their real workload. That is not usually true. 2.4.1 Login nodes Login nodes are used for: editing files navigating directories loading modules compiling small programs moving data submitting jobs Login nodes are not the place for serious computation. 2.4.2 Compute nodes Compute nodes are used for: running scheduled jobs interactive compute sessions parallel programs training or simulation workloads large production tasks 2.4.3 Rule of thumb Do setup on the login node. Do computation on compute nodes. That single idea prevents many of the worst beginner mistakes. 2.5 A simple cluster workflow Here is the workflow you will repeat throughout the course: Laptop | | ssh v Login node | edit files, inspect directories, load modules | write job scripts | | sbatch v SLURM scheduler | v Compute node(s) | v Output files and results The scheduler in this workflow is SLURM, which will become the center of Chapters 3 and 4. 2.6 Connecting to LS6 A standard SSH connection looks like this: ssh your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu After login, you may see a shell prompt like: [username@login1 ~]$ This prompt tells you several things at once: your username the machine name your current directory The prompt is not decoration. It gives you context. 2.7 Storage areas you will encounter Cluster workflows usually involve more than one storage location. 2.7.1 $HOME Your home directory is a personal, relatively safe place for configuration files, small scripts, and lightweight persistent material. 2.7.2 $WORK This is often a project-oriented area where you keep larger files, datasets, software builds, and materials that need to persist longer than temporary scratch storage. 2.7.3 $SCRATCH This is usually a high-performance temporary storage area. It is useful for active computation and temporary job data, but it should not be treated as permanent archival storage. 2.8 Why directories matter so much in HPC On a laptop, users often rely on clicking around to find files. In an HPC workflow, your current directory strongly influences: where your job looks for input where output files are written whether commands run on the files you intended That is why pwd and ls show up constantly in beginner workflows. 2.9 Minimal Linux toolkit for starting HPC work You do not need to master Linux before beginning. You do need a small set of reliable commands. pwd ls cd mkdir cp mv rm nano chmod +x These commands are enough to support the first real workflow in the course. 2.10 First exercise: create a workspace mkdir hpc_course cd hpc_course pwd ls This does four useful things: creates a directory for your course work moves you into that directory verifies where you are confirms what is inside it A surprising number of job failures come from skipping these checks. 2.11 First shell script Create a file: nano hello.sh Add: echo "Hello from LS6" Make it executable: chmod +x hello.sh Run it: ./hello.sh 2.12 What just happened? Even this small example teaches several important HPC habits. You: created a script file made the file executable ran a lightweight command safely on a login node confirmed that your shell environment is working This is appropriate login-node work because the task is tiny and brief. 2.13 Common beginner mistakes in Chapter 1 2.13.1 Running heavy work on the login node This is the most common conceptual mistake. Users often think, “I am logged in, so I can start the analysis.” In HPC, logging in gives you access to the environment, not ownership of shared computational resources. 2.13.2 Losing track of the current directory If you do not know where you are, you do not really know what your job will act on. 2.13.3 Forgetting execute permission A file can exist and still fail to run. ls -l and chmod +x are basic troubleshooting tools. 2.13.4 Treating storage areas as interchangeable Different storage areas serve different purposes. That matters for both performance and data safety. 2.14 Chapter lab Log in to LS6. Create a directory named hpc_course. Move into it and verify with pwd. Create hello.sh. Add one echo command. Make the script executable. Run it. Create a second script that prints your username, current directory, and date. Suggested commands: whoami pwd date 2.15 Reflection questions Why is a login node not the right place for serious computation? What is the difference between logging in and actually running a job? Why does your current directory matter before job submission? Which storage location would you trust for temporary high-volume output, and which would you treat more carefully? 2.16 Looking ahead In the next chapter, we slow down and build the Linux and environment skills that make later SLURM work easier. The theme of Chapter 2 is simple: good HPC habits begin with filesystem awareness and environment awareness.

Chapter 3 Linux Fundamentals and Environment Awareness 3.1 Overview This chapter builds the Linux foundation needed for real HPC work. Many job failures that appear to be “cluster problems” are actually basic environment problems: wrong directory missing file missing execute permission wrong module environment command not found By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: interpret the output of ls -l change file permissions for common cases inspect and reason about environment variables understand what PATH does load, list, and unload modules use a practical debugging checklist before submitting work 3.2 Mental model: an HPC system is a shared environment On a personal laptop, users often assume that their preferred software, file layout, and editor are just “there.” On an HPC system, the environment is more explicit. You are working in a space that is: multi-user resource-managed environment-driven structured for repeatability That means your workflow should be deliberate. You should know: where you are what files are present what software is loaded what commands are available in your environment 3.3 Inspecting the filesystem The first two commands to trust are: pwd ls -l 3.3.1 pwd Prints the current working directory. 3.3.2 ls -l Lists files with useful details such as permissions, owner, group, size, and modification time. An example permission string looks like this: -rwxr-xr-- A good beginner interpretation is: the file exists it has an owner, group, and permission settings some users may execute it, some may not 3.4 File permissions in practice Permissions matter constantly in Linux-based HPC workflows. You do not need a full theory of Unix security to start working productively. You do need the operational basics. 3.4.1 Common actions Make a file executable: chmod +x script.sh Remove write permission: chmod -w file.txt Use numeric mode: chmod 755 script.sh A helpful mental model for 755 is: owner: read, write, execute group: read, execute others: read, execute 3.5 Why permissions matter on clusters Common failures include: a script exists but will not execute a file cannot be edited because it is not writable a shared file has the wrong permissions for collaborators When a script “should work” but does not, ls -l is one of the fastest checks you can make. 3.6 Environment variables Environment variables define part of the context in which commands run. View them with: env Two important examples are: echo $HOME echo $PATH 3.6.1 $HOME Usually points to your home directory. 3.6.2 $PATH Tells the shell where to search for executable programs. If a program is not on your PATH, typing its name may produce: command not found 3.7 A practical mental model for PATH Think of PATH as a list of directories the shell searches when you enter a command name. If gcc is on your PATH, you can type: gcc --version If not, the shell may not know where to find it. A temporary way to add your own directory is: export PATH=$HOME/bin:$PATH This prepends $HOME/bin to the front of the search path for the current shell session. 3.8 Modules: software is part of the environment Clusters often use a module system rather than making every software package globally visible at all times. That means software is often loaded into the environment when needed. Common commands: module avail module load gcc module list module unload gcc 3.9 Why modules matter On an HPC system, different projects may need: different compiler versions different MPI libraries different Python stacks different container runtimes Modules help manage this complexity. Instead of assuming “the system version” is correct, users load an appropriate environment for the job. 3.10 Example: loading a compiler module load gcc which gcc gcc --version This sequence is valuable because it does three things: loads the compiler module shows which executable will run shows the version now active in the shell 3.11 Practical file inspection tools A few file-related commands are worth using early. Find a file: find . -name "example.txt" View a file: cat example.txt less example.txt head example.txt tail example.txt These tools are especially helpful when checking logs, outputs, and small configuration files. 3.12 Piping commands together One of the most useful Linux habits is combining small commands. cat file.txt | grep "error" This is a classic pipeline: one command produces output, the next filters it. You do not need to become an expert in shell pipelines immediately, but you should recognize that Linux tools are designed to be chained together. 3.13 Disk awareness matters HPC users need at least basic awareness of storage usage. Check filesystem space: df -h Check directory sizes: du -sh * These commands help prevent confusion when jobs fail because of full storage areas or because output is larger than expected. 3.14 Process awareness Sometimes you need to inspect or stop your own processes. List your processes: ps -u $USER Kill a process: kill <pid> This becomes more useful later when dealing with editors, accidental runs, or interactive sessions. 3.15 Optional connection to R For students who use R heavily, it is useful to remember that environment information can also be inspected from inside R. Sys.getenv("PATH") And shell commands can be invoked from R when appropriate: system("echo $HOME") That said, it is still important to understand the shell environment directly rather than relying only on IDE tools. 3.16 A debugging mindset for Linux-based HPC work When something goes wrong, start with evidence. Ask these five questions in order: 3.16.1 1. Where am I? pwd 3.16.2 2. What files are here? ls -l 3.16.3 3. Do I have the right permissions? ls -l script.sh 3.16.4 4. Is the program actually available? which gcc 3.16.5 5. Is my environment what I think it is? module list This checklist will reappear in later chapters because it prevents premature guessing. 3.17 Common beginner mistakes in Chapter 2 forgetting chmod +x assuming PATH is correct without checking forgetting to load a needed module filling up $HOME and wondering why workflows behave strangely treating missing commands as mysterious system failures instead of environment issues 3.18 Chapter lab 3.18.1 Part 1: permissions touch test.sh ls -l chmod +x test.sh ls -l 3.18.2 Part 2: environment variables echo $HOME echo $PATH export COURSE=HPC echo $COURSE 3.18.3 Part 3: modules module avail module load gcc module list which gcc gcc --version 3.18.4 Part 4: storage awareness df -h du -sh ~ 3.18.5 Part 5: file discovery touch example.txt find . -name "example.txt" 3.19 Stretch goal Create a custom bin/ directory in your home space, add it to PATH, place a small script in that directory, and run it without typing the full path. 3.20 Looking ahead The next chapter turns these Linux and environment skills into a real HPC workflow. Once you can reliably manage directories, permissions, and modules, you are ready to write a SLURM script and submit work to the scheduler.

Chapter 4 SLURM Job Scheduling on LS6 4.1 Overview On an HPC system, real computational work is normally submitted through a scheduler rather than run directly from the login node. On LS6, that scheduler is SLURM. This chapter introduces the practical workflow for batch jobs: write a job script request resources submit with sbatch monitor with squeue inspect finished jobs with sacct read output files and diagnose failures By the end of the chapter, you should be able to submit and interpret your first real SLURM job. 4.2 Mental model: SLURM is a resource manager Think of SLURM as the system that coordinates fair access to shared compute resources. You do not simply “grab a node.” Instead, you make a request that describes: what you want to run how many resources you need how long you need them which queue or partition should be used SLURM then decides when and where your job can run. 4.3 Why clusters use schedulers Without a scheduler: users would compete chaotically for resources jobs would interfere with each other there would be little accountability for resource use With a scheduler: jobs are queued resources are allocated intentionally usage is tracked the system remains usable for many people at once 4.4 A basic batch workflow You write a job script ↓ Submit with sbatch ↓ Job enters SLURM queue ↓ Scheduler allocates compute resources ↓ Job runs on compute node(s) ↓ Output and job history become available This is the operational heart of beginner HPC work. 4.5 Your first SLURM script Create a file: nano job.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J hello_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Hello from compute node" hostname date 4.6 Reading the script line by line 4.6.1 Shebang #!/bin/bash Tells the system to execute the script using Bash. 4.6.2 Job name #SBATCH -J hello_job Gives the job a readable name. 4.6.3 Nodes #SBATCH -N 1 Requests one node. 4.6.4 Tasks #SBATCH -n 1 Requests one task. 4.6.5 Wall time #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 Sets the maximum runtime to one minute. 4.6.6 Partition #SBATCH -p normal Requests the normal partition. Even at this stage, the main idea is becoming visible: a SLURM script is both a command script and a resource request. 4.7 Submit the job sbatch job.slurm Typical response: Submitted batch job 123456 That message does not mean your job has already run. It means the job has been accepted by the scheduler. 4.8 One of the most important beginner corrections Students often think: I used sbatch, so my program must already be running. That is not necessarily true. sbatch submits the job to the queue. The scheduler then determines when the job can start. 4.9 Monitoring queued and running jobs Use: squeue -u $USER This lets you inspect your current jobs. Typical fields include: job ID partition job name state elapsed time nodes used 4.10 Common SLURM job states PD = pending R = running CG = completing CD = completed A pending job is not broken simply because it is pending. It may be waiting for resources. 4.11 Output files as evidence When a batch job finishes, a file like this often appears: slurm-123456.out Inspect it with: cat slurm-123456.out or: cat slurm-*.out These output files are often the first place to look during debugging. 4.12 What just happened in the first example? When the simple hello_job runs, you have successfully completed an authentic cluster workflow: wrote a batch script requested resources submitted the request to SLURM allowed the scheduler to place the job executed commands on a compute node collected output from the run That is the core pattern you will repeat again and again. 4.13 Seeing the distinction between login and compute nodes Run hostname on the login node and compare it with the hostname printed inside a SLURM job. That difference makes the cluster architecture concrete. It helps students see that login access is not the same as compute allocation. 4.14 A longer-running observation job To observe a job in the queue or running state, edit the script: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J hello_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:02:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Starting job" hostname sleep 30 echo "Finishing job" Submit it again: sbatch job.slurm Then check: squeue -u $USER This lets you see the difference between job submission and job completion. 4.15 Looking backward with job accounting Jobs do not stay in squeue forever. Once they finish, you often want history rather than live status. Use: sacct To focus on a specific job: sacct -j <jobid> This is valuable for post-run diagnosis. 4.16 Cancelling a job If a job was submitted incorrectly or no longer needed, use: scancel <jobid> Common reasons to cancel a job include: wrong script bad resource request runaway test submission discovering an error immediately after submission 4.17 Common failure patterns 4.17.1 1. The job never starts Possible reasons include: unrealistic resource request wrong partition excessive wall time waiting for shared resources 4.17.2 2. The job fails immediately Possible reasons include: typo in a command missing module missing input file bad script logic 4.17.3 3. No output appears where you expected Possible reasons include: the job is still pending you are looking in the wrong directory output was redirected elsewhere 4.18 Debug from evidence, not guessing When a job seems wrong, use this sequence. 4.18.1 1. Did it submit? squeue -u $USER 4.18.2 2. Did it run or finish? sacct 4.18.3 3. What does the output file say? cat slurm-*.out 4.18.4 4. Was the environment correct? Check the job script for module load lines and inspect assumptions. 4.18.5 5. Was the job launched from the correct working directory? pwd ls This checklist prevents you from inventing explanations too early. 4.19 Failure-based learning exercise Try breaking a script intentionally in one of these ways: misspell a command request an unrealistic combination of resources refer to a file that does not exist load a module that is unavailable Then diagnose the result using: squeue sacct the output file inspection of the script itself This exercise is valuable because most HPC competence grows through interpreting evidence after a failure. 4.20 Chapter lab 4.20.1 Part 1: first batch job Create job.slurm. Add the minimal example. Submit with sbatch job.slurm. Monitor with squeue -u $USER. Inspect the output file. 4.20.2 Part 2: observe a running job Add sleep 30, resubmit, and watch the state change. 4.20.3 Part 3: change the resource request Change from one task to four tasks and compare behavior. 4.20.4 Part 4: load a module inside the job Add: module load gcc which gcc gcc --version Submit and inspect the output. 4.21 Best practices to keep now keep scripts small and readable name jobs clearly request realistic time and resources inspect the output after every run save working scripts rather than rebuilding them from memory 4.22 Looking ahead Once students can submit and monitor jobs confidently, the next natural question is: what exactly are we running on the cluster, and how do we build it? The next chapter moves from job submission to compiling C programs and understanding toolchains.

Chapter 5 Modules, Compilers, and Your First C Program 5.1 Overview 5.2 Mental model: source code becomes an executable 5.3 Why compilation matters on clusters 5.4 Two compilers you will hear about often 5.5 Modules first, then compilation 5.6 Confirm which compiler you are using 5.7 Your first C program 5.8 Compile the program 5.9 Run the executable 5.10 Inspect what was created 5.11 What just happened? 5.12 Optimization flags 5.13 A brief note on C++ 5.14 Multiple-file programs 5.15 Header files 5.16 Automating builds with make 5.17 Where compilation belongs in the workflow 5.18 Run compiled code through SLURM 5.19 Common beginner failures 5.20 Debug checklist for compilation work 5.21 Chapter lab 5.22 Looking ahead

Chapter 5 Modules, Compilers, and Your First C Program 5.1 Overview In this chapter, we move from running shell commands and SLURM scripts to building executable software from source code. This matters because many HPC workflows involve custom code, research software built from source, and toolchains that depend on specific compiler environments. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: explain what compilation does load a compiler module verify which compiler is active compile a small C program with gcc run the resulting executable prepare compiled code for SLURM execution 5.2 Mental model: source code becomes an executable A compiled workflow looks like this: source code (.c) ↓ compiler (gcc) ↓ executable binary ↓ run on LS6 That simple picture explains a great deal of later HPC practice. source code is human-readable the compiler translates source into machine-executable form the executable is what the system can actually run 5.3 Why compilation matters on clusters Compilation matters in HPC because: software is not always preinstalled the compiler version can affect behavior libraries and toolchains must often match each other performance can depend strongly on how code is built For beginners, the main goal is not advanced optimization. It is understanding the workflow clearly. 5.4 Two compilers you will hear about often gcc for C g++ for C++ This chapter focuses on C and gcc, because it gives the cleanest first compiled example. 5.5 Modules first, then compilation Before compiling, load a compiler environment: module avail module load gcc module list This reinforces a central HPC idea: software is part of the environment. 5.6 Confirm which compiler you are using which gcc gcc --version This is important because it replaces guessing with evidence. You now know: whether gcc is available where the executable is coming from which version is active 5.7 Your first C program Create the file: nano hello.c Add: #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello from C on LS6\n"); return 0; } This program does very little, but it demonstrates the full compiled workflow. 5.8 Compile the program gcc hello.c -o hello Interpretation: hello.c is the source file -o hello names the output executable hello 5.9 Run the executable ./hello Expected output: Hello from C on LS6 5.10 Inspect what was created ls -l You should see at least: hello.c hello This is an important moment for beginners: the source file and the executable are not the same thing. 5.11 What just happened? You: wrote source code invoked a compiler created an executable file ran that executable from the shell That is the core compiled workflow used across much of scientific computing. 5.12 Optimization flags A common form is: gcc hello.c -o hello -O2 Useful levels to know: -O0 = no optimization -O2 = standard practical choice -O3 = more aggressive optimization For an introductory course, -O2 is a good default example. 5.13 A brief note on C++ A similar pattern exists for C++: g++ hello.cpp -o hello_cpp The overall workflow is the same. The compiler changes because the language changes. 5.14 Multiple-file programs Real projects often have more than one source file. For example: main.c helper.c helper.h A simple compile step might be: gcc main.c helper.c -o program This introduces an important idea: programs can be built from multiple components. 5.15 Header files Headers (.h) are used to define interfaces or declarations. Example: int add(int a, int b); Even if students do not yet write large C projects, it helps to see that compilation can involve several files working together. 5.16 Automating builds with make When builds become repetitive, use a Makefile. Create: nano Makefile Add: all: gcc hello.c -o hello Then run: make The point of this early example is not to teach all of make, but to show how build steps can be automated and standardized. 5.17 Where compilation belongs in the workflow Good beginner rule: compile on login nodes for small builds run heavier or production computations through SLURM This keeps the workflow aligned with the earlier distinction between setup and computation. 5.18 Run compiled code through SLURM Create run.slurm: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J compile_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal ./hello Submit it: sbatch run.slurm Now the compiled workflow connects directly back to the scheduler workflow from the previous chapter. 5.19 Common beginner failures forgot module load gcc typo in the source code forgot -o hello tried to run from the wrong directory assumed compilation worked without reading the compiler output 5.20 Debug checklist for compilation work Use this order: module list which gcc ls -l gcc hello.c -o hello ./hello If the compile step fails, read the compiler message slowly. It is often telling you exactly where the problem is. 5.21 Chapter lab 5.21.1 Part 1: compile a C program Create hello.c. Compile with gcc hello.c -o hello. Run ./hello. 5.21.2 Part 2: inspect the files Use ls -l and note the difference between source and executable. 5.21.3 Part 3: use optimization Compile with: gcc hello.c -o hello_opt -O2 5.21.4 Part 4: run through SLURM Create run.slurm, submit it, and inspect the output file. 5.21.5 Part 5: optional Makefile Create the minimal Makefile and use make. 5.22 Looking ahead The next major conceptual jump is from serial execution to parallel execution. Once you understand how to compile and run one program, you are ready to ask how that work can be spread across multiple cores, threads, or nodes using MPI and OpenMP.

Chapter 6 Parallel Computing with MPI and OpenMP 6.1 Overview In this chapter, we move from single-core programs to parallel computing. You will learn: the difference between serial and parallel execution the difference between processes and threads how MPI and OpenMP work how to run parallel jobs with SLURM how to think about performance and scaling 6.2 Mental Model: Why Parallel Computing? A single core can only do one thing at a time. Parallel computing allows you to: split a problem into pieces solve those pieces simultaneously combine the results 6.3 Visual: Serial vs Parallel Serial: Task1 → Task2 → Task3 → Task4 Parallel: Task1 Task2 → run at the same time Task3 Task4 6.4 Two Main Models 6.4.1 MPI (Message Passing Interface) multiple processes can run across multiple nodes processes communicate via messages 6.4.2 OpenMP multiple threads usually within one node shared memory model 6.5 Mental Model: Processes vs Threads MPI: Process 1 → separate memory Process 2 → separate memory OpenMP: Thread 1 → shared memory Thread 2 → shared memory 🧠 Key Insight MPI is for distributed computing across nodes. OpenMP is for shared memory within a node. 6.6 Loading MPI module load openmpi 6.7 MPI Hello World (C) Create a file: nano hello_mpi.c Add: #include <mpi.h> #include <stdio.h> int main(int argc, char** argv) { MPI_Init(&argc, &argv); int rank, size; MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank); MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size); printf("Hello from process %d of %d\n", rank, size); MPI_Finalize(); return 0; } 6.8 Compile MPI Program mpicc hello_mpi.c -o hello_mpi 6.9 Run Locally for Testing mpirun -np 4 ./hello_mpi ⚠️ Important On HPC systems, production runs should go through SLURM. 6.10 MPI Job Script Create: nano mpi.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J mpi_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal module load openmpi mpirun ./hello_mpi Submit: sbatch mpi.slurm 6.11 Expected Output Hello from process 0 of 4 Hello from process 1 of 4 Hello from process 2 of 4 Hello from process 3 of 4 6.12 OpenMP Example Create: nano hello_omp.c Add: #include <stdio.h> #include <omp.h> int main() { #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); printf("Hello from thread %d\n", id); } return 0; } 6.13 Compile OpenMP Program gcc -fopenmp hello_omp.c -o hello_omp 6.14 Run OpenMP export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp 6.15 OpenMP SLURM Script Create: nano omp.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J omp_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -c 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp Submit: sbatch omp.slurm 6.16 Hybrid Concept You can combine MPI and OpenMP: MPI across nodes OpenMP within nodes 6.17 Mental Model Node 1: Process 1 → Threads Process 2 → Threads Node 2: Process 3 → Threads Process 4 → Threads 6.18 Performance Thinking We care about how runtime changes as cores increase. 6.18.1 Strong Scaling Same problem size, more cores. 6.18.2 Weak Scaling Problem size increases with cores. 6.18.3 Simple Timing Example time ./hello 💡 Insight More cores do not always mean faster results. Communication overhead matters. 6.19 Common Mistakes using MPI without loading the module mismatch between -n and program expectations forgetting OMP_NUM_THREADS running MPI directly on the login node misunderstanding cores vs tasks 6.20 Debugging Checklist 6.20.1 Check job: squeue -u $USER 6.20.2 Check output: cat slurm-*.out 6.20.3 Check modules: module list 6.20.4 Check resource request: nodes (-N) tasks (-n) cores (-c) 6.21 Lab 5 — Parallel Computing 6.21.1 Part 1: MPI Create hello_mpi.c Compile: mpicc hello_mpi.c -o hello_mpi Create SLURM script Run with 4 tasks 6.21.2 Part 2: OpenMP Create hello_omp.c Compile: gcc -fopenmp hello_omp.c -o hello_omp Run with: export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 6.21.3 Part 3: Compare run serial version run MPI version run OpenMP version 6.21.4 Part 4: Scaling Experiment Run MPI with different task counts and compare behavior and runtime. 6.22 Stretch Goal modify program to do actual computation measure runtime differences plot speedup 6.23 Transition You now understand: how to compile code how to run jobs how to use multiple cores Next, we modernize workflows with containers and reproducibility. 6.24 Key Takeaways Parallel computing splits work across cores MPI uses processes across nodes OpenMP uses threads within a node SLURM controls resource allocation Performance depends on scaling and overhead
containers-and-reproducible-hpc.html

Chapter 7 Containers and Reproducible HPC 7.1 Overview 7.2 Mental Model: What is a Container? 7.3 Visual: Container Concept 7.4 Why Containers Matter in HPC 7.5 Docker vs Apptainer 7.6 Mental Model 7.7 Checking for Apptainer 7.8 Pulling a Container 7.9 Running a Container 7.10 Interactive Shell 7.11 Mental Model: What Just Happened? 7.12 Running Python in a Container 7.13 Binding Directories 7.14 SLURM + Containers 7.15 Running Applications in Containers 7.16 Comparing Workflows 7.17 Reproducibility 7.18 Common Mistakes 7.19 Storage Considerations 7.20 Debugging Containers 7.21 Lab 6 — Containers on HPC 7.22 Stretch Goal 7.23 Transition to Capstone 7.24 Key Takeaways

Chapter 7 Containers and Reproducible HPC 7.1 Overview In this chapter, we introduce containers as a tool for reproducibility and portability in HPC. You will learn: what containers are and why they matter the difference between Docker and HPC containers how Apptainer works how to run containers on HPC systems how to integrate containers with SLURM 7.2 Mental Model: What is a Container? A container packages: your program all required libraries system dependencies environment configuration 7.3 Visual: Container Concept Your Program + Libraries + Dependencies + Environment ----------------- Container Image 7.4 Why Containers Matter in HPC Without containers: dependency conflicts occur software versions differ between systems workflows are hard to reproduce With containers: environments are consistent workflows are portable results are reproducible 🧠 Key Insight Containers solve the “it works on my machine” problem. 7.5 Docker vs Apptainer 7.5.1 Docker widely used in industry typically requires privileges not appropriate on shared HPC systems better suited to development and deployment workflows outside the cluster 7.5.2 Apptainer designed for HPC runs without root access in normal use integrates with SLURM commonly available on clusters 7.6 Mental Model Docker → development environment Apptainer → HPC execution environment 7.7 Checking for Apptainer which apptainer or: module load apptainer 7.8 Pulling a Container apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu 7.9 Running a Container apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Hello from container" 7.10 Interactive Shell apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.11 Mental Model: What Just Happened? you downloaded a container image you executed a command inside it the container used the host kernel 7.12 Running Python in a Container apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python3 --version 7.13 Binding Directories apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif ls 💡 Tip Always bind your working directory when running containerized jobs. 7.14 SLURM + Containers Create: nano container.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J container_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal module load apptainer apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Running inside container" Submit: sbatch container.slurm Inspect output: cat slurm-*.out 7.15 Running Applications in Containers Example: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python script.py 7.16 Comparing Workflows 7.16.1 Without container load modules install dependencies manage versions 7.16.2 With container run container everything is included in the image or explicitly bound 7.17 Reproducibility Containers allow you to: rerun experiments later share environments with others ensure consistency across systems 7.18 Common Mistakes forgetting to load the Apptainer module not binding directories assuming the container has every needed tool installed using Docker commands directly on the cluster storing large images in $HOME 7.19 Storage Considerations Store containers in: $WORK $SCRATCH Avoid: $HOME for large image files 7.20 Debugging Containers 7.20.1 Check image exists ls *.sif 7.20.2 Test interactively apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.20.3 Check a command apptainer exec ubuntu.sif which python3 7.21 Lab 6 — Containers on HPC 7.21.1 Part 1: Pull Image apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu 7.21.2 Part 2: Run Command apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Hello container" 7.21.3 Part 3: Interactive Mode apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.21.4 Part 4: SLURM Job Create container.slurm and run the container through SLURM. 7.21.5 Part 5: Bind Directory apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif ls 7.22 Stretch Goal run a Python script inside a container compare container vs native environment document differences 7.23 Transition to Capstone You now understand: system access Linux fundamentals job scheduling compiling software parallel computing container workflows 7.24 Key Takeaways Containers package complete environments Apptainer is designed for HPC systems Containers improve reproducibility SLURM can run containerized jobs Containers simplify complex workflows

Chapter 8 Capstone Project: End-to-End HPC Workflow 8.1 Overview 8.2 Project Philosophy 8.3 General Requirements 8.4 Project Options 8.5 Option 1: Compile and Run 8.6 Option 2: Parallel Computing 8.7 Option 3: Containerized Workflow 8.8 Option 4: Hybrid Workflow (Advanced) 8.9 Deliverables 8.10 Suggested Directory Structure 8.11 Reproducibility Requirements 8.12 Resource Awareness 8.13 Debugging Reflection 8.14 Grading Rubric 8.15 Example Workflow Summary 8.16 Common Mistakes 8.17 Stretch Goals 8.18 Final Reflection 8.19 Key Takeaways

Chapter 8 Capstone Project: End-to-End HPC Workflow 8.1 Overview The capstone project is the final component of this course. In this project, you will demonstrate your ability to: work effectively in a Linux-based HPC environment manage software environments using modules and/or containers compile and run programs submit and monitor SLURM jobs use parallel computing where appropriate document and reproduce your workflow 8.2 Project Philosophy This project is not just about “getting something to run.” It is about: reproducibility clarity resource awareness debugging ability 🧠 Key Insight A successful HPC user is not just someone who runs code, but someone who can reproduce and explain their workflow. 8.3 General Requirements All projects must include: a working directory structure source code or scripts at least one SLURM job script output files from completed jobs documentation describing the workflow 8.4 Project Options You may choose one of the following project types. 8.5 Option 1: Compile and Run 8.5.1 Description Compile a C or C++ program and run it on the HPC system. 8.5.2 Requirements write or obtain a C/C++ program compile using gcc or g++ create a SLURM job script run the program through SLURM capture and analyze output 8.5.3 Suggested Extensions use multiple source files create a Makefile compare optimized vs non-optimized builds 8.6 Option 2: Parallel Computing 8.6.1 Description Run a parallel program using MPI or OpenMP. 8.6.2 Requirements compile an MPI or OpenMP program run with multiple cores use SLURM to request resources demonstrate scaling behavior 8.6.3 Suggested Extensions compare serial vs parallel performance run with different core counts interpret speedup results 8.7 Option 3: Containerized Workflow 8.7.1 Description Run an application inside a container on the HPC system. 8.7.2 Requirements pull or use a container image execute commands using Apptainer run a containerized job via SLURM demonstrate reproducibility 8.7.3 Suggested Extensions bind directories correctly compare container vs native environment document dependencies 8.8 Option 4: Hybrid Workflow (Advanced) 8.8.1 Description Combine multiple techniques: compiled code parallel execution containerization 8.8.2 Requirements compile a program run it in parallel with MPI or OpenMP optionally execute within a container submit through SLURM 8.9 Deliverables Your submission must include: 8.9.1 1. Code and Scripts source files (.c, .cpp, .py, etc.) SLURM job scripts (.slurm) optional Makefile 8.9.2 2. Output Files SLURM output files (slurm-*.out) any generated results 8.9.3 3. Documentation A short report that explains: what you built how you compiled it how you ran it what resources you requested what output you observed what problems you encountered how you debugged them 💡 Tip Include command snippets in your report to make your workflow reproducible. 8.10 Suggested Directory Structure project/ ├── src/ ├── scripts/ ├── jobs/ ├── output/ └── README.md 8.11 Reproducibility Requirements Your project should be runnable by another user. This means: all steps are documented required modules are listed scripts are complete paths are clear ⚠️ Common Failure “It works on my account” is not sufficient. 8.12 Resource Awareness You should demonstrate understanding of: number of cores requested wall time memory, if relevant 8.13 Debugging Reflection You must include at least one example of: a failure you encountered how you diagnosed it how you fixed it 8.14 Grading Rubric 8.14.1 Correctness (30%) program runs successfully output is produced SLURM job completes 8.14.2 Workflow Clarity (25%) steps are documented commands are clear structure is organized 8.14.3 Reproducibility (20%) another user could repeat your process environment is clearly defined 8.14.4 Resource Usage (15%) appropriate use of cores and time understanding of SLURM directives 8.14.5 Reflection (10%) discussion of challenges debugging explanation 8.15 Example Workflow Summary A strong project might include: Write a C program Compile with gcc -O2 Create a SLURM script Run the job Observe output Modify for parallel execution Compare performance Document the workflow 8.16 Common Mistakes missing SLURM script no documentation unclear directory structure hardcoded paths no debugging explanation incorrect resource requests 8.17 Stretch Goals For advanced students: run multi-node MPI jobs build software with dependencies integrate containers with parallel jobs measure and plot performance 8.18 Final Reflection At this point, you have learned to: navigate an HPC system manage Linux environments submit and monitor jobs compile and run software use parallel computing work with containers 8.19 Key Takeaways HPC workflows involve multiple layers reproducibility is critical SLURM is central to execution environment management matters debugging is part of the process

Chapter 9 Appendix: HPC Quick Reference 9.1 Overview 9.2 SSH 9.3 Basic Linux 9.4 Permissions 9.5 Environment and Modules 9.6 SLURM 9.7 Compilation 9.8 MPI 9.9 OpenMP 9.10 Containers 9.11 Final Notes

Chapter 9 Appendix: HPC Quick Reference 9.1 Overview This appendix provides a compact quick reference for common commands and workflow patterns used throughout the course. 9.2 SSH ssh your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu 9.3 Basic Linux pwd ls -l cd mkdir project cp file1 file2 mv old new rm file 9.4 Permissions chmod +x script.sh chmod 755 script.sh 9.5 Environment and Modules echo $PATH module avail module load gcc module list module unload gcc 9.6 SLURM sbatch job.slurm squeue -u $USER sacct scancel <jobid> 9.7 Compilation gcc program.c -o program -O2 g++ program.cpp -o program make 9.8 MPI module load openmpi mpicc program.c -o program 9.9 OpenMP gcc -fopenmp program.c -o program export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 9.10 Containers module load apptainer apptainer exec image.sif command 9.11 Final Notes This appendix provides a quick reference for common HPC workflows. For full explanations and examples, refer back to the main chapters.
